title: Similarity & transformations
description: Given a pair of figures in the coordinate plane, determine whether they are similar based on whether it is possible to map one to the other using angle-preserving transformations.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bastian was able to map quadrilateral $OPQR$ onto $ONML$ using a reflection and a dilation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Bastian concluded:  

"I was able to map quadrilateral $OPQR$ onto quadrilateral $ONML$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the figures **are** similar."

**What error did Bastian make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map quadrilateral $OPQR$ onto quadrilateral $ONML$, then the figures are **similar**.

Bastian successfully mapped $OPQR$ onto $ONML$. Most importantly, he did this using a reflection, which is a rigid transformation, and a dilation. So the figures are similar.



[[☃ image 1]]

Bastian concluded:  

"I was able to map quadrilateral $OPQR$ onto quadrilateral $ONML$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the figures **are** similar."

**There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.**

options.alt: Quadrilateral O N M L and quadrilateral O P Q R in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. In quadrilateral O N M L, point O is at six. four, point N is at four, eight, point M is at zero, two, and point L is at two, zero. In quadrilateral O P Q R, point O is at six. four, point P is at seven, six, point Q is at nine, three, and point R is at eight, two. There is a dashed outline for quadrilateral O P Q R to show its image after a reflection over a vertical line that goes through point O. There are rays from the dashed outline for quadrilateral O P Q R to highlight that a dilation makes the new quadrilateral O P Q R coincide with quadrilateral O N M L.

choices.content: Bastian used a reflection, so the quadrilaterals *aren't* similar.
Bastian used a dilation, so the quadrilaterals *aren't* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Erin was able to map line segment $\overline{AB}$ onto line segment $\overline{CB}$ using a rotation and a dilation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Erin concluded:  

"I was able to map line segment $\overline{AB}$ onto line segment $\overline{CB}$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the figures **are** similar."

**What error did Erin make in her conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map line segment $\overline{AB}$ onto line segment $\overline{CB}$, then the figures are **similar**.

Erin successfully mapped line segment $\overline{AB}$ onto line segment $\overline{CB}$. Most importantly, she did this using a rotation, which is a rigid transformation, and a dilation. So the figures are similar.



[[☃ image 1]]

Erin concluded:  

"I was able to map line segment $\overline{AB}$ onto line segment $\overline{CB}$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the figures **are** similar."

**There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.**

options.alt: Quadrilateral O N M L and quadrilateral O P Q R in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. In quadrilateral O N M L, point O is at seven, five, point N is at five, nine, point M is at one, three, and point L is at three, one. In quadrilateral O P Q R, point O is at seven. five, point P is at eight, seven, point Q is at ten, four, and point R is at nine, three. There is a dashed outline for quadrilateral O P Q R to show its image after a reflection over a vertical line that goes through point O. There are rays from the dashed outline for quadrilateral O P Q R to highlight that a dilation makes the new quadrilateral O P Q R coincide with quadrilateral O N M L.

choices.content: Erin used a dilation, so the segments *aren't* similar.
The segments are *congruent*, not similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Eleanor was curious if triangles $\triangle DEF$ and $\triangle GHI$ were similar, so she tried to map one figure onto the other using rigid transformations.



[[☃ image 1]]

Eleanor concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle GHI$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the triangles are **not** similar."

**What error did Eleanor make in her conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle GHI$, then the figures are **similar**.

There are many possible sequences of transformations that may map one figure onto another.

Eleanor translated point $D$ onto point $G$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Is there another transformation (rigid or dilation) we could now use to complete the mapping?

Let's try dilating by a factor of $\dfrac12$ about point $G$.



[[☃ image 1]]

The pre-image $\triangle DEF$ maps exactly onto $\triangle GHI$.

Eleanor concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle GHI$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the triangles are **not** similar."

**One more transformation — a dilation — would map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle GHI$. So the triangles *are* similar.**

options.alt: Triangle D E F and triangle G H I in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. In triangle D E F, point D is at zero, zero, point E is at two, four, and point F is at six, zero. In triangle G H I, point G is at four, five, point H is are five, seven, and point I is at seven, five. There is also an image of triangle D E G after a translation which has a point at four, five, another point at six, nine, and another point at ten, five. There are arrows connecting the points from D E F to the points of the image after it's been translated.

choices.content: One more transformation — a rotation — would map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle GHI$. So the triangles *are* similar.
One more transformation — a dilation — would map $\triangle DEF$ onto $\triangle GHI$. So the triangles *are* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Casey was able to map line segment $\overline{UV}$ onto line segment $\overline{ST}$ using a translation and a rotation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Casey concluded:  

"I was able to map line segment $\overline{UV}$ onto line segment $\overline{ST}$ using a sequence of rigid transformations without a dilation, so the figures are congruent but they are **not** similar."

**What error did Casey make in her conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map line segment $\overline{UV}$ onto line segment $\overline{ST}$, then the figures are **similar**.

Casey successfully mapped line segment $\overline{UV}$ onto line segment $\overline{ST}$. Most importantly, she did this using a translation and a rotation, which are rigid transformations. So the figures are similar. [[☃ explanation 1]]



[[☃ image 1]]

Casey concluded:  

"I was able to map line segment $\overline{UV}$ onto line segment $\overline{ST}$ using a sequence of rigid transformations without a dilation, so the figures are congruent but they are **not** similar."

**The correct conclusion is that the segments are congruent *and* similar.**

options.alt: Line segments U V and S T are in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. In line segment U V, point U is at five, four. Point V is at seven, seven. In line segment S T, point S is at one, three and point T is at four, one. There is also an dashed image of segment U V after a translation which has a point at one, three and another point at three, six. There are arrows connecting the points from U and V to the points of the image after it's been translated. The image is them rotated to overlay segment S T.

choices.content: The correct conclusion is that the segments are congruent *and* similar.
The correct conclusion is that the segments are *neither* congruent, *nor* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Hüseyin was able to map quadrilateral $EFGH$ onto $EJGK$ using a vertical stretch.



[[☃ image 1]]

Hüseyin concluded:  

"I was able to map quadrilateral $EFGH$ onto quadrilateral $EJGK$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the figures **are** similar."

**What error did Hüseyin make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map quadrilateral $EFGH$ onto quadrilateral $EJGK$, then the figures are **similar**.

Hüseyin successfully mapped quadrilateral $EFGH$ onto quadrilateral $EJGK$, but he didn't use rigid transformations and dilations. He used a vertical stretch, which changes both distances and angle measures.

[[☃ image 1]]

Hüseyin concluded:

"I was able to map quadrilateral $EFGH$ onto quadrilateral $EJGK$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the figures **are** similar."

**Hüseyin used a vertical stretch, so the quadrilaterals *aren't* similar. **

options.alt: Quadrilateral E F G H and quadrilateral E J G K. In quadrilateral E F G H, point E is at zero, six, point F is at six, twelve, point G is at twelve, six, and point H is at six, zero. In quadrilateral E J G K, point E is at zero, six, point J is at six, eight, point G is at twelve, six, and point K is at six, four. There are arrows from point F to point J and from point H to point K to represent the movement of these points from the vertical stretch.

choices.content: Hüseyin used a vertical stretch, so the quadrilaterals *aren't* similar.
The quadrilaterals are mapped onto each other, so they are congruent, *not* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Konnor was curious if quadrilaterals $STUV$ and $WXYZ$ were similar, so he considered their angles.



[[☃ image 1]]

Konnor concluded:  

"The quadrilaterals have four pairs of congruent corresponding angles, so the figures **are** similar."

**What error did Konnor make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map quadrilateral $STUV$ onto quadrilateral $WXYZ$, then the figures are **similar**.

Konnor did not try to map $STUV$ onto $WXYZ$ (or vice versa). Instead, he only looked at their angles measures.



[[☃ image 1]]

However, two quadrilaterals having the same angles doesn't guarantee they are similar (the converse is true, though: if they are similar, they have the same angles).

These two quadrilaterals aren't similar because you can't map them using rigid transformations and dilations. You must stretch $STUV$ horizontally so $\overline{TU}$ and $\overline{SV}$ are longer while $\overline{TS}$ and $\overline{UV}$ stay the same length. This cannot be done with rigid transformations or dilations.

Konnor concluded:  

"The quadrilaterals have four pairs of congruent corresponding angles, so the figures **are** similar."

**It's impossible to map $STUV$ onto $WXYZ$ using only rigid transformations and dilations, so the figures are *not* similar.**

options.alt: Quadrilateral S T U V and quadrilateral W X Y Z. In quadrilateral S T U V, point S is at two, seven, point T is at two, nine, point U is at five, nine, and point V is at seven, seven. In quadrilateral W X Y Z, point W is at two, two, point X is at two, four, point Y is at six, four, and point Z is at eight, two. Angles U and Y are one hundred thirty-five degrees. Angles V and Z are forty-five degrees.

choices.content: The quadrilaterals are congruent, not similar.
It's impossible to map $STUV$ onto $WXYZ$ using only rigid transformations and dilations, so the figures are *not* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lennox was curious if triangles $\triangle ABE$ and $\triangle DCE$ were similar, so she tried to map one figure onto the other using a reflection and a dilation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Lennox concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $\triangle DCE$ onto $\triangle ABE$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the triangles are **not** similar."

**What error did Lennox make in her conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map $\triangle DCE$ onto $\triangle ABE$, then the figures are **similar**.

Notice that both triangles are right triangles (at vertices $B$ and $C$), with $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{DC}$ being the longer legs.

Therefore, if the triangles are similar, we should be able to map each pair of corresponding points onto each other with rigid transformations and dilations:

- $D$ should be mapped onto $A$.
- $C$ should be mapped onto $B$.
- $E$ should be mapped onto itself.



[[☃ image 1]]

Lennox used a reflection across line $\overleftrightarrow{BE}$ and a dilation about $E$ to get $\triangle DCE$ as close as possible to $\triangle ABE$. But still, point $D$ did not map onto point $A$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So we can conclude that it's not possible to map $\triangle DCE$ onto $\triangle ABE$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations.

Lennox concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $\triangle DCE$ onto $\triangle ABE$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the triangles are **not** similar."

**There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.**

options.alt: Triangle A B E and triangle D C E. In triangle A B E, point A is at one, four, point B is at six, four, and point E is at six, zero. In triangle D C E, point D is at nine, two, point C is at six, two, and point E is at six, zero. There is an image for triangle D C E after it's reflected over side C E. The only new point for the image is point D which moves to three, two. There is also arrows to show the dilation of triangle D C E. One arrow goes from point D on the image to the point zero, four. Another arrow goes from Point C on the image to six, four.

choices.content: One more transformation — a horizontal stretch — would map $\triangle DCE$ onto $\triangle ABE$. So the triangles *are* similar.
One more transformation — a dilation — would map $\triangle DCE$ onto $\triangle ABE$. So the triangles *are* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Timo was curious if quadrilaterals $LMNR$ and $PQNO$ were similar, so he tried to map one figure onto the other using a rotation and a dilation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Timo concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $LMNR$ onto $PQNO$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the quadrilaterals are **not** similar."

**What error did Timo make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map $LMNR$ onto $PQNO$, then the figures are **similar**.

Notice that both quadrilaterals are kites, with $\overline{MR}$ and $\overline{QO}$ being the longer diagonals, $R$ and $O$ connecting the longer pair of sides, and $N$ is a shared vertex.

Therefore, if the quadrilaterals are similar, we should be able to map each pair of corresponding points onto each other with rigid transformations and dilations:

- $L$ should be mapped onto $P$.
- $M$ should be mapped onto $Q$.
- $N$ should be mapped onto itself.
- $R$ should be mapped onto $O$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Timo used a $180\degree$ rotation about $N$ and a dilation about $N$ to get $LMNR$ as close as possible to $PQNO$. But still, point $M$ did not map onto point $Q$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So we can conclude that it's not possible to map $LMNR$ onto $PQNO$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations.

Timo concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $LMNR$ onto $PQNO$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and dilations, so the quadrilaterals are **not** similar."

**There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.**

options.alt: Quadrilateral L M N R and quadrilateral P Q N O  in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. In quadrilateral L M N R, point L is at two, five, point M is at four, six, point N is at six, five, and point R is at four, two. In quadrilateral P Q N O, point P is at fourteen. five, point Q is at ten, two, point N is at six, five and point O is at ten, eleven. There is a dashed outline for quadrilateral L M N R to show its image after a rotation of one hundred eighty degrees about point N and a dilation about point N where point N prime is at six, five, M prime is at ten, three, L prime is at fourteen, five, and R prime is at ten, eleven.

choices.content: One more transformation — a vertical stretch — would map $LMNR$ onto $PQNO$. So the quadrilaterals *are* similar.
One more transformation — a dilation — would map $LMNR$ onto $PQNO$. So the quadrilaterals *are* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the similarity statement.**

A rectangle is [[☃ dropdown 1]] similar to another rectangle, because we can [[☃ dropdown 2]] map one onto the other using only dilations and rigid transformations.
hints: A pair of figures is similar if we can map one onto the other using only rigid transformations and dilations.

Some rectangles are similar to each other. For example, you can map the smaller rectangle below onto the larger rectangle by dilating it by a factor of $2$ about point $P$.


[[☃ image 1]]

Some rectangles are not similar to each other. For example, you can't map rectangle $ABCD$ onto rectangle $ABEF$ using rigid transformations and dilations alone. In order to map $ABCD$ onto $ABEF$ you must increase its height without changing its width. A dilation can only change both dimensions.



[[☃ image 1]]
In conclusion, a rectangle is **sometimes** similar to another rectangle, because we can **sometimes** map one onto the other using only dilations and rigid transformations.

choices.content: sometimes
always
never
sometimes
always
never

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the similarity statement.**

An equilateral triangle is [[☃ dropdown 1]] similar to a scalene triangle, because we can [[☃ dropdown 2]] map one onto the other using only dilations and rigid transformations.
hints:
A pair of figures is similar if we can map one onto the other using only rigid transformations and dilations.

All rigid transformations and all dilations preserve angle measure.

An equilateral triangle always has three congruent $60\degree$ angles.

A scalene triangle always has three different angle measures because it has three different side lengths. So even if one of the angles in a scalene triangle is $60\degree$, the other two aren't.
Rigid transformations and dilations preserve angle measure, so they can never map a $60\degree$ angle onto an angle with a different measure.
An equilateral triangle is **never** similar to a scalene triangle, because we can **never** map one onto the other using only dilations and rigid transformations.

choices.content: sometimes
always
never
sometimes
always
never

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the similarity statement.**

A line segment is [[☃ dropdown 1]] similar to another line segment, because we can [[☃ dropdown 2]] map one onto the other using only dilations and rigid transformations.
hints:
A pair of figures is similar if we can map one onto the other using only rigid transformations and dilations.

Consider line segments $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$. Let's see if we can map $\overline{AB}$ onto $\overline{CD}$ using only rigid transformations and dilations.

If we succeed, we will think whether that's possible for *any* pair of segments.



[[☃ image 1]]

First, we use a *translation* so $B'$, the image of point $B$, is on point $D$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Then we use a *rotation* about point $D$ so $A''$, the image of point $A'$, is on the line $\overleftrightarrow{CD}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Finally, we use a *dilation* about point $D$. We choose our scale factor so that $A'''$, the image of point $A''$, is on point $C$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So we were successful at mapping $\overline{AB}$ onto $\overline{CD}$ using only rigid transformations and a dilation.

In fact, we can use a similar process to map any line segment to any line segment. [[☃ explanation 1]]
A line segment is **always** similar to another line segment, because we can **always** map one onto the other using only dilations and rigid transformations.

choices.content: sometimes
always
never
sometimes
always
never

assessment_items.question_content:
Sabrina was able to map circle $P$ onto circle $R$ (with $Q$ and $S$ being on each circle, respectively) using a translation and a dilation.
 

[[☃ image 1]]

Sabrina concluded:  

"I was able to map circle $P$ onto circle $R$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and a dilation, so the figures **are** similar."

**What error did Sabrina make in her conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map circle $P$ onto circle $R$, then the figures are **similar**.

Sabrina successfully mapped circle $P$ onto circle $R$. Most importantly, she did this using a translation, which is a rigid transformation, and a dilation. So the figures are similar.



[[☃ image 1]]

Sabrina concluded:

"I was able to map circle $P$ onto circle $R$ using a sequence of rigid transformations and a dilation, so the figures **are** similar."

**There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.**

options.alt: Two circles. The first circle has a center P with a radius that goes to a point on the circle at point Q. The second circle has center R with a radius that goes to a point at point S. The second circle's radius is larger than the first circle's radius. The first circle's center P prime is mapped onto the second circle's center R before being dilated to map completely onto the second circle.

choices.content: Sabrina used a translation, so the circles *aren't* similar.
Sabrina used a dilation, so the circles *aren't* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Pedro was curious if circles $A$ and $B$ (with $X$ and $Y$ being on each circle, respectively) were similar, so he tried to map one figure onto the other using rigid transformations.



[[☃ image 1]]

Pedro concluded:  

"It's not possible to map circle $A$ onto circle $B$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the circles are **not** similar."

**What error did Pedro make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) and dilations can map circle $A$ onto circle $B$, then the figures are **similar**.

There are many possible sequences of transformations that may map one figure onto another.

Pedro performed a translation so point $A$ coincides with point $B$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Is there another transformation (rigid or dilation) we could now use to complete the mapping?

Let's try dilating by a factor of $\dfrac12$ about point $B$.



[[☃ image 1]]

The pre-image, circle $A$, maps exactly onto circle $B$.

Pedro concluded:  

"It's not possible to map circle $A$ onto circle $B$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the circles are **not** similar."

**One more transformation — a dilation — would map circle $A$ onto circle $B$. So the circles *are* similar.**

options.alt: Two circles A and B in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The first circle has a center A at seven, three with a radius of two units and a point X on the circle at nine, three. The second circle has a center B at three, seven with a radius of one unit and a point Y on the circle at three, eight. The first circle is translated four units left and four units up to map center A prime onto center B. Point X prime is at five, seven.

choices.content: One more transformation — a rotation — would map circle $A$ onto circle $B$. So the circles *are* similar.
One more transformation — a dilation — would map circle $A$ onto circle $B$. So the circles *are* similar.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the similarity statement.**

A circle is [[☃ dropdown 1]] similar to another circle, because we can [[☃ dropdown 2]] map one onto the other using only dilations and rigid transformations.
hints:
A pair of figures is similar if we can map one onto the other using only rigid transformations and dilations.

Consider circles $A$ and $B$ ($P$ is a point on circle $A$). Let's see if we can map circle $A$ onto circle $B$ using only rigid transformations and dilations.

If we succeed, we will think whether that's possible for *any* pair of circles.



[[☃ image 1]]

First, we use a *translation* so $A'$, the image of point $A$, is on point $B$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Then, we use a *dilation* about point $B$. We choose our scale factor so that $P''$, the image of point $P'$, is on circle $B$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So we were successful at mapping circle $A$ onto circle $B$ using only rigid transformations and a dilation.

In fact, we can use a similar process to map any circle to any other circle. [[☃ explanation 1]]
A circle is **always** similar to another circle, because we can **always** map one onto the other using only dilations and rigid transformations.

choices.content: sometimes
always
never
sometimes
always
never
